title: Non-overlapping Intervals
code: Array.Sort(intervals, (a, b) => a[1].CompareTo(b[1]));   // by end time
if (iv[0] >= end)            // compatible with the last kept interval
return intervals.Length - kept;  // intervals we had to remove

frames.caption: Start with 4 intervals: [1,2] [2,3] [3,4] [1,3]. The greedy needs them ordered by END time.
frames.note: We track just two scalars: `kept` (how many mutually-compatible intervals we have selected) and `end` (the finish time of the most recent kept interval), seeded to -infinity so the very first interval is always compatible. No DP table, no per-interval bookkeeping — O(1) space beyond the sort.

frames.caption: Sort by end time ascending (ends 2,3,3,4): [1,2] [1,3] [2,3] [3,4]. Earliest finish first.
frames.note: Sorting by END time ascending is the entire algorithm — an exchange argument proves it optimal: the interval that FINISHES earliest leaves the most room for everything after it, so swapping any optimal first pick for the earliest-finishing one never lowers how many fit. Sorting by START time can be sabotaged by one long early interval that finishes late and blocks several short ones. After the O(n log n) sort the rest is a single O(1)-state sweep.

frames.caption: Initialize kept = 0, end = -infinity. No interval kept yet, so the first one is always compatible.
frames.note: We track just two scalars: `kept` (how many mutually-compatible intervals we have selected) and `end` (the finish time of the most recent kept interval), seeded to -infinity so the very first interval is always compatible. No DP table, no per-interval bookkeeping — O(1) space beyond the sort.

frames.caption: [1,2]: start 1 >= end -inf -> KEEP. kept = 1, end = 2.
frames.note: The test is `iv.start >= end`, where `end` is the finish time of the LAST interval we kept. If the new interval starts at or after that, it is compatible — it cannot overlap the kept one — so we keep it and advance `end` to its finish time. Greedily keeping the earliest-finishing compatible interval maximises the count kept, and `kept` is the only running state we need.

frames.caption: [1,3]: start 1 < end 2 -> overlaps the last kept interval, REMOVE it. kept stays 1.
frames.note: When `iv.start < end` the new interval overlaps the last one we kept, so the two cannot both survive. We greedily DROP this one (not the kept interval) because the kept interval finishes no later, so keeping it never forecloses a future choice. Every interval we skip here is exactly one we must remove — that is what the final `total - kept` counts.

frames.caption: [2,3]: start 2 >= end 2 -> KEEP. kept = 2, end = 3.
frames.note: The test is `iv.start >= end`, where `end` is the finish time of the LAST interval we kept. If the new interval starts at or after that, it is compatible — it cannot overlap the kept one — so we keep it and advance `end` to its finish time. Greedily keeping the earliest-finishing compatible interval maximises the count kept, and `kept` is the only running state we need.

frames.caption: [3,4]: start 3 >= end 3 -> KEEP. kept = 3, end = 4.
frames.note: The test is `iv.start >= end`, where `end` is the finish time of the LAST interval we kept. If the new interval starts at or after that, it is compatible — it cannot overlap the kept one — so we keep it and advance `end` to its finish time. Greedily keeping the earliest-finishing compatible interval maximises the count kept, and `kept` is the only running state we need.

frames.caption: Kept 3 compatible intervals out of 4. Answer = total - kept = 4 - 3 = 1 removal.
frames.note: The answer to LC 435 is `total - kept`: we removed exactly the intervals we could not keep. Keeping the MAXIMUM number of compatible intervals (the classic activity-selection result) is identical to removing the MINIMUM number — so this greedy is optimal. Total cost is O(n log n) for the sort, then O(n) for the one-pass scan.
